{{Short description="Spontaneous transformation of an unstable atomic nucleus into a different nucleus}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Radioactive decay
| image = Alpha Decay.svg
| caption = [[Alpha decay]]: a heavy nucleus emits an [[alpha particle]] (helium-4 nucleus).
| topics = [[Nuclear physics]], [[Atomic nucleus]], [[Alpha decay]], [[Beta decay]], [[Gamma ray]]
}}

'''Radioactive decay''' is the spontaneous transformation of an unstable [[atomic nucleus]] into a different nucleus, accompanied by the emission of [[particle]]s or [[electromagnetic radiation]]. This process changes the original atom (the "parent") into a different [[chemical element|element]] (the "daughter").

== Types of decay ==

=== Alpha decay ===
{{Main|Alpha decay}}
Emission of an [[alpha particle]] (<math>^4\text{He}^{2+}</math>):
:<math>^{238}_{92}\text{U} \rightarrow ^{234}_{90}\text{Th} + ^4_2\text{He}</math>
* Atomic number decreases by 2, mass number by 4
* Common in heavy nuclei (Z > 82)

=== Beta decay ===
{{Main|Beta decay}}
* '''β⁻''': neutron → proton + electron + antineutrino
:<math>n \rightarrow p + e^- + \bar{\nu}_e</math>
* '''β⁺''': proton → neutron + positron + neutrino
:<math>p \rightarrow n + e^+ + \nu_e</math>
* '''Electron capture''': proton + inner electron → neutron + neutrino

=== Gamma decay ===
Emission of high-energy [[photon]]s ([[gamma ray]]s) from an excited nucleus:
:<math>^{60}_{27}\text{Co}^* \rightarrow ^{60}_{27}\text{Co} + \gamma</math>
* No change in atomic or mass number
* Often follows alpha or beta decay

=== Spontaneous fission ===
Heavy nucleus splits into two medium-mass nuclei without external trigger.

== Decay law ==
Radioactive decay is a statistical process governed by:

:<math>N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}</math>

where <math>\lambda</math> is the decay constant. The [[half-life]] is:

:<math>t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}</math>

== Activity ==
The activity <math>A</math> is the number of decays per unit time:

:<math>A = \lambda N</math>
Measured in [[becquerel]] (Bq): 1 Bq = 1 decay per second.

== Applications ==
* [[Radiocarbon dating]] (<sup>14</sup>C, <math>t_{1/2} = 5,730</math> yr)
* [[Nuclear medicine]] — diagnostic and therapeutic isotopes
* [[Radiotherapy]] — cancer treatment
* [[Smoke detector]]s — <sup>241</sup>Am alpha source
* Geological dating — [[uranium–lead dating]]

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Alpha decay]]
* [[Beta decay]]
* [[Gamma ray]]
* [[Half-life]]
* [[Nuclear physics]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Radioactivity]]
[[Category:Nuclear physics]]
